title: Mission 02 — Deteriorating Weather
summary: A VFR cross-country with lowering ceilings, visibility pressure, and weather reroute decisions. This mission teaches when to stop pressing and start diverting.

weather.summary: A warm front is lowering ceilings westbound, with Louisville trending toward marginal VFR and light rain showers.

notams: KLOU runway 24 wet braking action advisories possible after 2000Z.
AIRMET Sierra valid west of Lexington for ceilings below 3000 feet.

personalMinimums: No ceilings below 3000 ft for solo cross-country
Always identify at least one VFR diversion airport

events.title: Weather Go / No-Go
events.prompt: Louisville is already trending toward marginal VFR and your personal minimums call for at least 3000-foot ceilings. What is the best preflight decision?

options.label: Delay, get an updated briefing, and be prepared to scrub if ceilings keep dropping.

consequence.feedback: Correct. The weather is near your stated minimums, so reassessment and flexibility are the best ADM choice.
consequence.commMessage: Checkride note: delaying for another briefing due to lowering ceilings near destination.

options.label: Launch immediately before it gets worse and hope Louisville stays usable.

consequence.feedback: Poor ADM. Time pressure and weather pressure are combining into a classic get-there-itis setup.
consequence.commMessage: Weather is close to minimums, but I’m launching anyway to beat the system.

options.label: Launch VFR and plan to just pick up IFR later if needed.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. You must plan within your current qualifications and the actual operational environment, not wishful escalation.
consequence.commMessage: I’ll sort it out in the air if the weather drops.

events.title: Diversion Planning
events.prompt: Which preflight action best prepares you for a weather-driven diversion on this route?

options.label: Brief Frankfort and Shelbyville as explicit diversion options with current weather and runway data.

consequence.feedback: Correct. A real diversion plan lowers workload when conditions begin to tighten.
consequence.commMessage: Primary outs briefed: KFFT and KJVY equivalents on route.

options.label: Skip alternate planning because the route is short and familiar.

consequence.feedback: Weak planning. Familiar routes still demand formal outs when the weather picture is degrading.

options.label: Carry extra fuel but don’t worry about specific diversion fields.

consequence.feedback: Extra fuel helps, but it does not replace actual diversion decision-making.

events.title: Fuel Strategy
events.prompt: With weather uncertainty increasing the chance of a diversion or delay, what fuel strategy is best?

options.label: Carry the planned 60 gallons so you have healthy legal and practical reserve if you divert.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Extra reserve buys time and options when weather is changing.
consequence.commMessage: Loaded with healthy reserve for a likely diversion scenario.

options.label: Launch with only legal minimum fuel because the route is under an hour.

consequence.feedback: Unsafe. Weather uncertainty is exactly when bare-minimum fuel becomes a trap.

options.label: Top all the way off even though you don’t need it and it adds unnecessary performance penalty.

consequence.feedback: Defensible, but not ideal. Match fuel to mission risk without creating unnecessary weight.

events.title: Lowering Ceilings Ahead
events.prompt: Approach reports rain shafts west of Shelbyville and ceilings dropping toward 2500 feet. What is the best action?

options.label: Turn toward your planned diversion while VFR options are still wide open.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Early diversion preserves options and demonstrates strong ADM.
consequence.weatherSummary: Ceilings continue lowering westbound; diverting early keeps you comfortably in VFR conditions.
consequence.commMessage: Approach, Skylane 734JP is diverting to the planned alternate due weather trend.

options.label: Continue another few minutes to see if the weather opens up.

consequence.feedback: Marginal. Waiting reduces your decision margin and can quickly turn into scud-running pressure.
consequence.weatherSummary: Visibility is holding for now, but the weather picture is tightening and your outs are shrinking.

options.label: Descend below the lowering cloud base to maintain visual contact with the ground.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Descending into shrinking margins is classic scud-running and a checkride bust move.
consequence.commMessage: Descending below the deck to keep the field in sight.

events.title: Special VFR Pressure
events.prompt: A passenger asks if you can just request Special VFR and finish the trip quickly. How do you respond?

options.label: Explain that your mission is to stay within personal minimums and continue only if the risk picture supports it.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Good ADM includes resisting external pressure and sticking to your standards.
consequence.commMessage: We’re staying with our minimums and not using passenger pressure as a planning tool.

options.label: Tell the passenger you’ll probably request Special VFR if the field drops below normal VFR.

consequence.feedback: Weak ADM. Special VFR is not a shortcut to salvage a poor weather decision for a checkride scenario.

options.label: Promise the passenger you’ll get them there one way or another.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. External pressure should be managed, not amplified.

events.title: After the Divert Decision
events.prompt: Once you decide to divert, what radio call best reflects good cockpit management?

options.label: Advise ATC of the diversion, request the new destination frequency or services, and reset the approach mentally.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Clear communication reduces confusion and helps you stabilize the new plan.
consequence.commMessage: Approach, Skylane 734JP diverting to Frankfort due weather, request advisories.

options.label: Quietly turn away and sort the rest out later.

consequence.feedback: Poor communication discipline. ATC and traffic awareness matter even in a diversion.

options.label: Cancel everything immediately even though you still need workload help and traffic awareness.

consequence.feedback: Not ideal. Services can help you manage the diversion unless there’s a clear reason to drop them.
